text: The video shows a right indirect inguinal hernia in the patient, visible as a protrusion in the groin area.
The surgeon identifies a segment of sigmoid epiploica adhered to the peritoneum medial to the inferior epigastric vessels and prepares to divide it.
The surgeon assesses the size of the inguinal hernia defect and evaluates the necessary overlap for mesh placement during the robotic tap inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon begins the peritoneal incision, preparing the surgical field for the robotic tap inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon begins creating a peritoneal flap, a step in the robotic tap inguinal hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon carefully navigates between the peritoneum and the transversalis fascia, maintaining a clear separation between these anatomical layers during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessels, visible within the surgical field.
The surgical view does not reveal the inferior pregastric vessels, indicating they are obscured or not yet exposed in the current field.
The surgeon enters the retro-rectus or pre-transversalis layer, revealing the anatomical structures within this space.
The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessels within the retro-rectus space, providing a clear view that aids in the surgical procedure.
The surgeon moves medially to access the space of Retzius, initiating the procedure with simpler steps.
The surgeon divides all layers of the transversalis fascia and the investing fascia, staying close to the peritoneum.
The surgeon carefully navigates laterally, hugging the peritoneum to maneuver around the sac, positioned between the sac and the testicular vessels.
The surgeon opens a layer referred to as the Shiozaki layer, revealing underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully separates the adipose tissue from the peritoneum, revealing the distinct layer between these structures.
The surgeon identifies the testicular vessels, which are visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to carefully dissect tissue, minimizing thermal spread and preserving surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses their left hand to retract tissue, revealing areolar tissue and confirming the correct surgical plane.
The surgeon works medially in the space of Retzius, maintaining proximity to the rectus muscle during dissection.
The surgeon divides the right medial umbilical ligament to create additional space for medial dissection.
The surgeon works on reducing the indirect inguinal hernia sac, manipulating the tissue to decrease its size within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery and applies pressure to locate the end of the indirect sac, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.
The surgeon prepares to cauterize small vessels in the surgical field, ensuring hemostasis and clear visibility of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses short bursts of cautery to maintain hemostasis while working off the sac, ensuring minimal bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon uses the left hand to pull the tissue to the nine o'clock position, effectively splaying out the layers in the back for better visualization.
The surgeon identifies the vas deferens, visually distinguishing it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the VAS and proceeds with more aggressive dissection, likely using surgical instruments to manipulate and separate tissue layers.
The surgeon uses short bursts of energy to delineate and visualize the different tissue planes, aiding in the separation of anatomical layers.
The surgeon visually identifies the edge of the sac and the location of the VAS difference, with clear visibility of these anatomical landmarks.
The surgeon, having identified the vas deferens, proceeds with more aggressive dissection around the inferior epigastric vessels, carefully navigating the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses the left hand to retract and pull the bladder and surrounding fat away from the rectus muscle, enhancing visibility and access to the surgical area.
The surgeon searches for the white areolar separation planes, which are visible as distinct tissue layers during the procedure.
The surgeon uses short bursts of energy, likely from an electrocautery device, while maintaining good retraction with the left hand to manipulate the tissue effectively.
The surgeon has the vas deferens in view, preparing for further dissection or manipulation.
The surgeon continues to peel away the peritoneum, revealing underlying anatomical structures and separation planes.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers instruments to avoid injuring the inferior gastric vessels, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the peritoneum, maintaining close proximity to its surface during dissection.
The surgeon applies a short burst of energy to delineate the tissue planes, enhancing the visibility of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully dissects deeper into the pelvic region, mindful of the external iliac vein and femoral vein, which could be pulled medially during the procedure.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field, searching for adipose tissue to confirm that the vein is not within the area being dissected.
The surgeon continues to free the peritoneum, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues to ensure clear visibility of the anatomical planes.
The surgeon begins dissecting through the natural tissue planes between the fat, carefully separating the layers to avoid the external iliac and femoral veins.
The surgeon uses scissors to gently push against the tissue, checking for the presence of underlying bone.
The surgeon uses an instrument to probe the tissue, visibly encountering resistance as it contacts the underlying bone.
The surgeon identifies a whitish structure, which may be the bladder, within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins superficial dissection, carefully avoiding deeper tissue layers to prevent complications.
The surgeon encounters scar tissue in the surgical field, which may require careful manipulation to proceed with the dissection.
The surgeon continues to work within the adipose tissue, ensuring the dissection remains within the fat layer.
The surgeon encounters an area where the tissue appears adhered or stuck, requiring careful manipulation to proceed.
The surgeon identifies the bladder within the surgical field, noting its proximity to the area being operated on.
The surgeon employs sharp dissection with brief bursts of cautery to navigate through scar tissue and fatty layers.
The surgeon identifies the bladder within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the bony structures of the pelvis, maintaining close proximity to these anatomical landmarks during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully applies minimal energy with the cautery device while working directly on the bladder to avoid damage.
The surgeon uses the left hand to apply traction, revealing a fine tissue plane that is typically present in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses the left hand to demonstrate the foamy areolar layer, while the right hand precisely cuts through dense tissue near the pubis. The bladder is clearly visible, and the surgeon pulls on it to maintain exposure, emphasizing the importance of hemostasis to control any bleeding.
The surgeon stops the bleeding and places a piece of gauze to absorb the blood, aiming to restore clear visibility of the tissue planes.
The surgeon continues the dissection, being mindful of the location of the femoral vein as they progress deeper into the tissue layers.
The surgeon exposes the white structure identified as the medial umbilical ligament, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully peels the peritoneum away from the testicular vessel, ensuring that the mesh can be positioned well posteriorly.
The surgeon identifies and displays what appears to be the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, highlighting its position relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon exposes the general femoral nerve adjacent to the iliac artery, highlighting the spatial relationship between these anatomical structures.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of the medial peritoneal opening (MPO), ensuring clear visualization of surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon exchanges the scissors for a mega suture cut needle driver, preparing to perform suturing or tissue manipulation.
The mesh is visible inside the abdomen, having been inserted prior to the docking of the robotic surgical system.
The surgeon displays a 15x10 cm piece of anatomical Pritex ProGrip mesh within the surgical field.
The surgeon folds the mesh, exposing the seam, and orients it within the surgical field based on the seam's alignment.
The surgeon positions the mesh with the seam approximately two centimeters away from the internal ring, ensuring proper alignment within the surgical field.
The surgeon unfolds the anatomical Pritex pro grip mesh within the abdominal cavity, ensuring it is properly oriented for placement.
The surgeon unfolds the anatomical Pritex Pro Grip mesh within the abdominal cavity, improving handling and positioning.
The surgeon exposes the lower flap of the anatomical Pritex Pro Grip mesh and carefully tucks it into the space of Retzius, positioning it against the pubis and Cooper's ligament.
The surgeon ensures that the mesh provides adequate posterior coverage over the anatomical structures.
The surgeon checks the posterior flap of the mesh to ensure there is no peritoneum sneaking in behind it.
The surgeon performs a routine closure of the mesh flap, ensuring it is securely positioned against the anatomical landmarks such as the pubis and Cooper's ligament.
The surgeon closes the flap while carefully maintaining control of the needle with one of the surgical instruments to prevent it from dropping.
The surgeon uses the left hand to pull the needle out, positioning it for the right hand to grasp it in preparation for suturing.
The surgeon performs two additional throws with the suture before cutting it, ensuring secure closure.
The surgeon pulls the suture tight, then relaxes it before cutting it flush to the peritoneum, completing the closure of the flap.
The surgeon closes the surgical site using several figure-of-eight sutures with 3-0 Vicryl, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated securely.
